question: Match the lines with the Figures of Speech. Lines 1. In wondrous merry mood. 2. They were so queer, so very queer. 3. And saw him peep within. 4. The grin grew broad. 5. And shot from ear to ear.
answer:
Lines
Figures of Speech
Explanation

1. In wondrous merry mood.
Transferred Epithet
The adjective “merry” describes the person’s state of mind, but it is transferred to the word “mood”.

2. They were so queer, so very queer.
Repetition
The words “so queer” are repeated for emphasis.

3. And saw him peep within.
Alliteration
The sound of the letter ‘w’ is repeated in “without” and “within” (implied context from Oliver Wendell Holmes’ poem The Height of the Ridiculous).

4. The grin grew broad.
Alliteration/Tautology
The ‘g’ sound is repeated in “grin grew”. (Note: In the context of this specific poem’s textbook exercises, this is often paired with Alliteration or tautology, depending on the specific answer key, but phonetically, it is alliteration.)

5. And shot from ear to ear.
Hyperbole
An exaggeration; a grin cannot literally shoot physically from one ear to the other.

6. He broke into a roar.
Onomatopoeia
The word “roar” imitates the actual sound being made.

7. Ten days and nights with sleepless eye.
Transferred Epithet/Hyperbole
“Sleepless” is transferred from the person to their “eye”. It is also a Hyperbole because staying awake for ten straight days and nights is a massive exaggeration.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.4 - The Height of the Ridiculous

question: Copy any two stanzas of the poem in the lines below. Using a coloured pen underline the stressed syllables in each line and put a stress-mark (') over each.
answer:
Stanza 1: 'I 'wrote some 'lines once 'on a 'time In 'won-drous 'mer-ry 'mood, And 'thought, as u-'su-al, 'men would 'say They were ex-'ceed-ing 'good.
Stanza 3: “'These to the 'print-er,” 'I ex-'claimed, And, 'in my 'hu-mor-ous 'way, 'I 'add-ed ('as a 'tri-fling 'jest,) “There’ll 'be the 'dev-il to 'pay.”
Explanation:

Stressed syllables are marked where the voice naturally emphasises sound (e.g., “I,” “wrote,” “lines”).
Students would underline these in their notebooks with a colored pen.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.4 - The Height of the Ridiculous

question: Form goups in your class and together compose a short humorous poem. Use jokes, experiences, etc. and convert it to a poetic form.
answer:
The Great Classroom Mystery
We walked into the classroom, ready for the day,
But realized with a horror, we forgot to text and say:
“Hey friend, did you complete the homework due by nine?”
My group members just stared at me, a shiver down their spine!
The teacher walked in smiling, with chalk upon her hand,
We started plotting fake excuses, the best in all the land.
“My dog ate up my notebook!” or “My internet went dead!”
But honestly, the truth was, we just stayed too long in bed.
Then came the sudden pop-quiz, the ultimate betrayal,
Our faces turned as white as ghosts, our confidence grew frail.
I looked across at Rahul, who was staring at the wall,
Hoping the answers would appear, or the ceiling just would fall!
We survived the class together, though our brains were turned to mush,
When the final bell rang loudly, you should have seen the rush!
We might not be the smartest, and our homework might be late,
But as a group of chaotic friends, we think we're doing great!
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.4 - The Height of the Ridiculous

question: Read the following poem and write an appreciation of it with the help of the given points in paragraph format. The Height of the Ridiculous I wrote some lines once on a time In wondrous merry mood
answer:
1Show SolutionThe poem The Height of the Ridiculous’ by Oliver Wendell Holmes is a humorous poem written for enjoyment. with plenty of funny expressions. The main purpose of the poet is to simply entertain his audience. The rhyme scheme of the poem is ‘abcb’. There are many figures of speech, like Hyperbole, Tautology,
Onomatopoeia, Alliteration, etc., but the one that stands out is the transferred epithet. In the line Ten days and nights, with sleepless eye the adjective sleepless should be for the man and not for the eye. This is a humorous poem. The special feature of the poem is the use of expressions that convey funny moments. The reader forms a mental picture and enjoys the poem. My favourite line is The sixth, he burst five buttons. I like this line because I can imagine the way the man must have been laughing till he burst the buttons on his coat. I like the poem because it is light, simple, and entertaining.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.4 - The Height of the Ridiculous

question: Pair up with your partner and answer the following question: Do you have a hero or a role model to look up to? Who is he/she?
answer: Yes, my role model is my grandmother. Though she has led a very difficult life because she was poor, she is always optimistic and sees the best in people. She is always cheerful and ready to learn new things. She has learned how to use a computer and surfs the internet regularly. She is even learning a new language with the help of the computer! She is health-conscious and does yoga regularly. I wish to be like her.

question: Pair up with your partner and answer the following question: Do you have a passion you would fight for? Why?
answer: Yes, my role model is Captain Prem Mathur, who obtained her pilot’s license in 1947, when the idea of gender equality was nonexistent in our country. In an era when people doubted the capabilities of a woman pilot, Mary Airlines rejected her. She was fired, kept trying, and finally, after a few years, was hired by Deccan Airlines. In 1949, she won the National Air Race.

question: Write a book review on any book of your choice with the help of the following points. Story line Social/Historical content Writing style My thought
answer:
The Room on the Roof by Ruskin Bond

Story line: The story in this book is about an orphaned boy named Rusty. He is a sad and lonely boy, living with his guardian, Mr. Harrison. Rusty, in his search for an identity, dares to venture out of the house and make new friends. He is finally left homeless by Harrison, and this is when he realises what it means to be an orphan in the truest sense. Rusty’s friend comes. to his rescue when he lands Rusty a job teaching English. Things are fine for a while, after which a strange sequence of events occurs, and Rusty ends up homeless again. This time, he is on the verge of leaving for England when his student, Kishen, stops him, and together they decide to start afresh in life on a new note.
Social/Historical content: The story about the Anglo-Indian teenager Rusty bears a striking resemblance to the life of its writer, Ruskin Bond. Despite Ruskin's pain at his parents’ separation and a lonely childhood, he developed an optimistic outlook on life much like Rusty does in the story. It is this teenager’s search for freedom, identity, and friends that creates the story's context and plot. The book was published in 1956, just after India acquired independence. It reflects on a time when a few English remained back in India for good. It depicts how life comes a complete circle for Rusty, from being lonely to being surrounded by friends and yet being lonely again. Only this time around, Rusty is wise enough to realise that he has the potential to begin life afresh.
Writing style: Ruskin Bond has a distinctly poetic writing style. His choice of words and placement of imagery provide for an interesting yet lucid read. Particularly fascinating is the narrative. When the boy finds himself torn between living in India and England. Ruskin is very descriptive and hence uses many adjectives to support his writing, which makes his works delightful to read. He has also cleverly conveyed in the novel how strangers can become part and parcel of Orie’s life by being kind and warm in their interactions. Rusty, the central character in the novel, uses this same idea to make friends for life.
My thought: I love this book because of its simple style and the picture it paints of a teenage boy I can relate to, since we are of the same generation. I also like the multiple emotions the author depicts beautifully in this piece. I would like to read other works of Ruskin. Bond, because of his expertise in making simple things beautiful.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.5 - The Old Man and The Sea: Book Review

question: Classroom Library: Ask all your classmates to donate a book each to the class. In this way everyone will have a book of his/her own to read. Read the book in a week or two.
answer:
To help your class launch this successfully and keep track of all the books, here is a step-by-step game plan you can share with your classmates or teacher.
Step-by-Step Launch Plan:

The Book Drive: Have everyone bring in one book they are willing to share for the school year. Label the inside cover with the owner’s name so it doesn’t get lost.
Build the Catalogue: List all the books on a sheet of paper or a simple spreadsheet. This is your “library catalogue,” so everyone knows what choices are available.
First Reading Round: Everyone picks a book to start with. Spend the next two weeks reading your chosen book during free periods or at home.
The Fortnightly Review: Hold your first presentation day! Share a quick thought about the book you just finished before handing it off to the next person.
The Great Exchange: Pass your finished book to a classmate who wants to read it next, check a new one off the catalogue, and repeat the cycle!
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.5 - The Old Man and The Sea: Book Review

chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Why did Jim sell his gold watch? Do you think he did well?
answer: Jim sold his gold watch, which belonged to his father and grandfather, to buy a set of beautiful combs that Della yearned for. Jim made a mistake by selling his watch to buy the combs because Della had already sold her hair to buy him a platinum chain for his gold watch.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Why did Jim and Della want to present the gifts?
answer: Jim and Della wanted to present the gifts to show their love and affection for each other. They wanted to make each other happy on Christmas.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Why did Della feel sad on losing her hair?
answer: Della felt sad because she loved her hair. It was a part of her beauty, and she felt less confident without it. She also felt that her husband might not love her the same without her beautiful hair.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: What did Jim bring out from his coat? Describe the scene.
answer: Jim brought out a small, beautifully wrapped package from his coat. Della was excited and curious. She opened it and found the combs she had always wanted, but then she burst into tears because she had no hair to wear them with. Jim was shocked and confused at first.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: How beautiful was the gold watch chain? Would you have liked to own it?
answer: The gold watch chain was described as perfect, and Jim was really excited about it. It sounds really beautiful, and I would love to own something so shiny and fancy.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: How did the Magi want to celebrate their Christmas (OR) Who were the first people to give Christmas gifts?
answer: The Magi were the first people to give Christmas gifts. They gave gifts to baby Jesus.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: What is the moral of the story?
answer: The moral of the story is that the true meaning of Christmas and gift-giving is love and sacrifice. It’s about giving without expecting anything in return. Jim and Della showed the ultimate act of love by sacrificing their most prized possessions for each other.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Map the problems and solutions in “The Gift of the Magi” on the diagram below. Two problems have already been filled in. Problem : Della doesn’t have enough money to buy Jim a present for Christmas.
answer: See solution on Shaalaa.com
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHAT ..... troubled Della on Christmas Eve?
answer: Della had only one dollar and eighty-seven cents with which to buy Jim a present. She had spent a lot of time thinking about a good present for him, but with the money she had saved, she could not find anything worthy of him.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHAT ..... was the current family income?
answer: Twenty dollars a week was the current family income.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHAT ...... could have outshone Queen of Sheba’s jewels?
answer: Della’s beautiful, long hair could have outshone the Queen of Sheba’s jewels.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHAT ...... was the cost of the fob chain?
answer: Twenty-one dollars was the cost of the fob chain.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHAT ...... sent Jim into a trance, on his return?
answer: The sight of Della’s short hair sent Jim into a trance on his return.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHY ...... did Della sell her lovely long hair?
answer: Della sold her lovely, long hair to buy a special Christmas gift for Jim, but she didn’t have enough money for anything worthwhile.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHY ..... was Della a bit apprehensive after she had sold her hair?
answer: Della was a bit apprehensive after selling her hair, as she loved it very much and was worried about how Jim would react to her new appearance.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHY ..... did Jim avoid checking the time in his gold watch, in a public place?
answer: Jim avoided checking the time in his gold watch, in a public place because the watch was old.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHY ..... did Della’s scream of joy change to loud wails?
answer: Della’s scream of joy changed to loud wails because she realised that the gift she had bought for Jim was useless, as he had sold his watch to buy her a gift.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHY ..... in the end, did Jim ask Della to put the chops on?
answer: In the end, did Jim ask Della to put the chops on because they had sacrificed their most prized possessions for each other and realized that the true gift was their love, not material possessions.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHEN ..... had Jim added ‘Dillingham’ as his middle name?
answer: When Jim was paid $ 30 per week, he added ‘Dillingham’ as his middle name.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHEN ..... did a tear or two drop on the old red carpet?
answer: Della made up her mind to cut off her beautiful hair.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHEN ..... did Della go through goods in the stores?
answer: day before Christmas, Della went through the goods in the store to find her husband a good gift.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHEN ..... did Della say a silent prayer to God?
answer: When Della had sold her beautiful hair and had come back to her home, she prayed to God.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Say WHEN .....did Jim try to divert their attention for ten seconds, at least?
answer: When Jim realised that Della was upset, seeing as Jim was shocked to see her new appearance with short hair.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Complete: Use the chart below to explain the situational irony in ‘The Gift of the Magi’. For each character, identify what is expected to happen and what actually happens.
answer:
SITUATIONAL IRONY

What Della plans: To give Jim a wonderful fob chain for Christmas.
What actually happens: Jim cannot use Della’s gift of a fob chain for his watch because he had already sold his watch to get her a Christmas gift.

What Jim plans: To give Della a wonderful Christmas gift in the form of a set of tortoise shell combs.

What actually happens: Della cannot use Jim’s gift of combs because she has cut and sold her hair to get him a Christmas gift.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Word List: agile cascade	coveted instigate prudence assertion chronicle inconsequential predominating subside gobbling Fill in each set of blanks with the correct word from the word list.

C A S C A D E

2. You wished greedily for something.
C O V E T E D

3. This describes something important.
P R E D O M I N A T I N G

4. You hope a loud siren will do this.
S U B S I D E

5. Troublemakers do this.
I N S T I G A T E

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. What would you do if you ran short of money, to buy a birthday gift for one of your parents/siblings?
answer: If I ran short of money to buy a gift for one of my parents/siblings, I would borrow from someone else close to me. For example, if I wanted to buy a bag for my sister and didn't have enough money, I would ask my mother to lend it to me. If I wanted to buy something for Mom and did not have the money, I would borrow it from Dad.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. Think of two alternatives that could have prevented Della as well as Jim from selling their most precious possessions.
answer: Both knew that they were poor and couldn’t afford expensive gifts. So they could have just given each other small token gifts of flowers or cards. This would have prevented them from selling their most precious possessions.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi
